data.title: Service Corporation International: Visible Demand, Solid Pricing Power, And Moat
data.description:
Investment Action

I give a buy rating for Service Corporation International (SCI) because it has a very visible demand profile over the medium term and strong pricing power. Demographic tailwinds support steady volume growth, high fixed costs create operating leverage, and the cemetery business provides a durable moat. On top of that, the stock is cheap relative to one of its peers, which gives comfort that multiples could expand.

Company Description

SCI is the largest deathcare operator in North America. It provides funeral, cremation, and cemetery services through a large network of funeral homes and cemeteries across the US, Canada, and Puerto Rico. It earns revenue in two ways: one is at the time of need, and two is via preneed contracts that are planned and paid for years in advance.

Demographics Trend Drive Predictable Volume Growth

The strongest growth driver for SCI is the demographics' tailwind. The Baby Boomer generation is now moving into its late 70s and early 80s, which is the stage of life where death rates go up dramatically (vs. when they are younger). More than 10,000 baby boomers retire every single day in the US, and about four baby boomers pass away every minute. That works out to about 2 million deaths a year. While we can't predict the timing of death, we know with 100% certainty that everyone will pass away eventually. (Not many humans live past 100 years, for perspective.) As such, this demographic shift should naturally drive death volumes for many years to come over the foreseeable future. Unlike most consumer businesses, demand here doesn’t depend on macro sentiment or income; it follows demographics, and that creates a floor for volume growth.

This volume growth matters a lot over these coming few years because SCI is largely a fixed-cost business. The buildings, staff, and operations are largely the same regardless of volume. As a result, even a small increase in volume could drive a larger magnitude of margin expansion.

Multi-Billion Dollar Preneed Backlog

We can get a sense of the demand runway by looking at SCI’s preneed backlog. SCI now has ~$16.8 billion of preneed contracts already sold. To be clear, this is revenue that has already been contracted and sales that have already been made (the cash is effectively secured, waiting to be recognized as revenue when the service is performed in the future). What matters here is the pace of backlog growth, which is ~2x vs. reported revenue growth. This massively raises the bar in terms of visibility, giving comfort that even in a recession, this revenue does not disappear, as these services have already been contracted (a person's timing of death has nothing to do with macro cycles).

The problem is whether SCI can leverage this cash position to grow the business. Since the timing of death is out of SCI’s hands, it cannot really “grow” demand by investing in marketing. Hence, I think management has made the right strategy to invest these funds into the market. About half of SCI’s $16.8 billion preneed backlog is held in trust funds, and the other half is backed by insurance contracts. This should help it grow the cash over time and keep up with inflation. The good news is that, historically, these investments have grown faster than funeral costs and even created extra profit when the service is ultimately delivered. For example, a family prepays $10,000 for a funeral. The trust grows to $14,000, the funeral costs $12,000, and the extra $2,000 becomes profit for SCI.

Real Estate Moat And Strategic Consolidation

This is largely a simple business with almost certain demand, but SCI has a strong moat that makes it hard to displace, and that moat lies in its cemetery segment. In major cities like New York, Los Angeles, and Houston, building a new cemetery is extremely difficult due to land scarcity and strict zoning rules (besides, nobody wants a cemetery besides them). What this means is that SCI’s 499 existing cemeteries are in near-monopoly positions in their local markets. And since these burial spaces are limited and cannot be easily replenished, SCI naturally has growing pricing power over time, stemming from (1) increased death volume and (2) rising land cost. This is very different from funeral services, where barriers to entry are much lower. Funeral homes can basically be operated from standard commercial buildings, which makes it easier for new operators to enter the market.

To work around that “gap,” SCI combines locations, meaning to say, it operates funeral homes and cemeteries on the same site. This changes the competitive dynamics, making it in favor of SCI. At these locations, families can arrange funeral services (such as embalming, paperwork, and memorials) and select a burial space in one visit. This one-stop setup makes it more likely SCI provides both the service and the burial, rather than losing part of the business (the funeral services) to competitors. Most independent operators own either a funeral home or a cemetery, but not both, and this is why I think SCI has a structural advantage.

Taking a step back, the broader deathcare industry is also highly fragmented, with ~80% still run by independent operators. Scale is important in this industry because cost can be better optimized, which means as SCI scales, its advantage over sub-scale players will only get wider. For example, after acquisition, SCI can reduce costs through centralized purchasing of items like caskets and urns. This puts it in a good position to roll up some of these fragmented players to further scale up, which drives the growth flywheel again. Since the fact is that demand is visible, this acquisition strategy is relatively low risk, in my view, as SCI can just target established, cash-generating businesses and improve them by reducing costs and raising margins.

Valuation

There are not many publicly available peers for SCI. The main ones that I could find are Propel Funeral Partners (listed in AU) and Carriage Services (CSV). They all have similar mid-single-digit organic revenue growth, but SCI has the highest margin (30% EBITDA margin vs. the other two at 24% and 27%, respectively) and is the largest in terms of scale (~$4.3 billion LTM revenue). In this context, I would think SCI deserves to trade at a premium, but it was surprising that Propel Funeral Partners traded at 28x forward PE (~9x premium vs. SCI). For historical context, SCI traded at an average of 20x over the past 10 years, with 24x as the high. Say that multiples go back to 20x (for whatever reasons, the market still discounts SCI relative to Propel), and adj. EPS comes in as consensus expected (historically accurate), the stock is worth ~$94 based on FY27 adj. EPS estimates.

Risk

While I am positive of SCI’s pricing power, if it cannot raise prices across funeral and cemetery services as I expected, margins may not expand by as much. Higher interest rates could also pressure near-term returns, as the timing of death (revenue recognition) is not guaranteed. There is risk in acquisitions if SCI overpays or struggles to integrate new assets.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating to SCI because it offers predictable, demographic-driven demand and strong pricing power from its cemetery assets. It is also cheap when compared to Propel Funeral Partners, which has a similar growth outlook but lower margin and scale.

data.title: Soquelitinib Data Push Corvus Pharmaceuticals Stock Higher: What You Should Know
data.description:
Thesis: Positive Soquelitinib Data Signals More Upside

Corvus Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (CRVS) lead asset would have to be soquelitinib. It's an investigational oral small-molecule drug being developed by Corvus. It's been seen to selectively inhibit interleukin-2–inducible T-cell kinase (ITK), which is a key signaling enzyme involved in T-cell activation and differentiation. The compound is being studied mainly for immune-mediated diseases and T-cell–driven cancers, which, of course, include atopic dermatitis and relapsed/refractory peripheral T-cell lymphoma. Mechanistically speaking, ITK inhibition acts upstream in the whole immune signaling cascade, which would shift T-cell differentiation away from pro-inflammatory Th2 and Th17 cells.

In theory, this shift should reduce allergic and autoimmune inflammation and promote regulatory and Th1 T cells. This should help out in rebalancing immune responses rather than just broadly suppressing them.

Impressive Results

As you know, Corvus reported some very encouraging new data. It pushed the stock price up a whopping 165%. The data is from cohort 4 of its randomized, blinded, placebo-controlled Phase 1 trial of soquelitinib. The trial was done on patients with moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis, and the data should set the company up nicely to advance its clinical profile of this investigational oral ITK inhibitor. Now, cohort 4 specifically was designed to extend treatment duration to up to eight weeks, compared with the previous four-week dosing we saw in cohorts 1 to 3. This trial also enrolled patients with much more advanced disease progression.

In fact, many of the participants had actually previously failed systemic therapies like dupilumab and JAK inhibitors. So, at day 56 of the trial, patients receiving soquelitinib in 200 mg twice daily doses managed to achieve a mean 72% reduction in EASI score versus just 40% in those with the placebo. There was also a clear and statistically significant separation of response curves emerging as early as day 15 of the trial. This response widened throughout the trial until the end of treatment.

The EASI score is the Eczema Area and Severity Index; it is used to see how bad the atopic dermatitis is and evaluates four major regions of the body in the head/neck, trunk, upper limbs, and lower limbs. Achieving EASI-75 would, for the most part, be considered a benchmark for meaningful clinical improvement, whilst hitting EASI-90, on the other hand, would reflect near-clearance of disease.

So, overall clinically meaningful response rates were notable, with 75% of soquelitinib-treated patients achieving EASI-75 and 25% managing to achieve EASI-90, which was very impressive. What was even more impressive, however, was that 33% reached IGA 0/1, which is the clinical endpoint that refers to no signs of atopic dermatitis at level 0 and minimal residual signs at level 1. These results were compared with 20%, 0%, and 0%, respectively, in the placebo group. Importantly, efficacy was consistent regardless of the patients' prior systemic treatment exposure, which goes to show there was activity here even in treatment-resistant patients.

Biomarker analyses also supported the proposed upstream immunomodulatory mechanism of ITK inhibition. We saw reductions in both Th2- and Th17-associated cytokines like IL-4, IL-5, IL-17, and TARC. There were also increases in regulatory T cells and some evidence of sustained disease control beyond just the treatment period, which was a nice sign.

Safety, in general, remained pretty favorable, with no serious/severe adverse events. There were also no dose interruptions, and only mild to moderate adverse events occurred at rates that were comparable to the placebo. Collectively speaking, these results are pretty impressive and would suggest that longer treatment with soquelitinib may even deepen and sustain the clinical responses we just saw. That's one reason why the market reaction was so bullish; this was only seen in an 8-week trial.

So now Corvus has to move into a Phase 2 trial with an even longer treatment period. Now, if we see similar or even more pronounced results similar to what we saw yesterday, I expect the stock to have a similar reaction. Many positives were priced in with the 165% stock jump, with the market now realising that this candidate has potential and a very big TAM, as I'll explain in a bit. Next, I think we should see an upcoming Phase 2 study in which patients sustain treatment for 12 weeks, followed by a 30-day off-treatment follow-up. This should allow investigators to evaluate not only the peak efficacy for soquelitinib but also the durability of response post-drug withdrawal. Durability will be a big factor moving forward.

Risks

So even with the strong clinical momentum we just saw, Corvus still remains a high-risk investment purely given the fact that soquelitinib is not yet approved and still faces a pretty long, uncertain development path. There is still pivotal data, larger patient populations to be tested, and eventually a regulatory review ahead. The company is also pre-revenue, so the current valuation is almost entirely dependent on future trial success. It makes the stock highly sensitive to clinical setbacks. We saw the stock price in about a 165% single-day surge following the data release, but a reversal of that could well be the case if we see negative news related to soquelitinib.

A Word on the TAM

Now, just to touch briefly on the TAM, as it is one of the main reasons for the heavy stock run-up. The atopic dermatitis drug market is currently quite substantial and would be a rapidly growing global opportunity for Corvus should it see more positive trial results from soquelitinib. Many industry forecasts estimate the global AD treatment market to be valued somewhere in the high teens of billions of U.S. dollars. Whilst some projections range from about $17 to $18 billion in 2024 to nearly $30 billion by 2030, growing at mid-to-high single-digit compound annual growth rates. Other analyses tip the market to approach $22 to $25 billion by the early 2030s or higher when you extend it to broader treatment categories and longer time horizons.

Either way you look at it, it is a pretty big opportunity for Corvus in a space where healthcare systems and payers are adopting premium biologic and systemic therapies if they can meaningfully improve long-term outcomes. As for that competition, approved therapies in the space command some pretty high annual costs. Biologic therapies like dupilumab/Dupixent, the treatment options some of the trial patients had previously failed to see results from, are a current standard of care for moderate-to-severe AD.

Now, dupilumab has historically been priced at about $25,000 per year in the U.S. market; however, the list prices per carton are sometimes cited to be closer to $4,193 with some big payer discounts or variations depending on one’s dosing. So various analyses place real net annual costs somewhere in the low-to-mid five-figure range, depending on regimen/indication.

Elsewhere, there are many oral JAK inhibitor treatment options, such as abrocitinib/Cibinqo. These options also represent some major competitors for Corvus with estimated annual drug costs in the $17,000 to $20,000 range. Which is again quite a meaningful expense relative to traditional topicals. Importantly, however, we need to think of how soquelitinib would sit in contrast to these established therapies, especially when it comes to dosing for patients. You see, the current therapies I mentioned need chronic administration. So we often see patients remain on biologics or JAK inhibitors for many years just to maintain disease control.

Even the real-world "drug survival" analyses show us that for biologics like dupilumab, substantial proportions of patients continue therapy at 1 to 2 years or even longer. When you think about the cost stacking up for multiyear treatments, the prices start getting extremely elevated. JAK inhibitors, on the other hand, tend to have somewhat higher discontinuation rates. Which is most probably due to effectiveness or tolerability issues.

With these kinds of prices, it's now very important that investors look out for soquelitinib durability of response studies. You see, the longer the response we see from extended dosing and even possible immune modulation, the better the potential soquelitinib has moving forward against the competition. I expect the next catalyst for Corvus stock to be dependent on that durability response.

Valuation

Corvus’ valuation would be best assessed using market cap and enterprise value, rather than any earnings-based multiples, given that the company is still in a pre-revenue, clinical-stage stage. They currently have no commercial products on the market. Their market cap, post stock run-up, is now sitting at about $1.6 billion with an enterprise value of about $1.53 billion. They also have very minimal debt of about $1.0 million.

The company currently does not generate any revenue, which, of course, makes traditional metrics meaningless. I think instead, investors are effectively valuing Corvus on the expected future value of soquelitinib we could see across the multiple indications, the most notable of which being atopic dermatitis and peripheral T-cell lymphoma.

Now, from a balance sheet/liquidity perspective, Corvus reported cash of about $65.7 million last quarter. And since they’re a clinical-stage company running multiple trials, Corvus’ cost structure, as you would expect, is dominated by research and development and general operating expenses. Based on its historical operating profile as a late Phase 1/Phase 3 oncology biotech, Corvus’ operating cash burn is about $20 to $25 million per quarter, or roughly $80 to $100 million annually. If we take this operating spend as a proxy for cash burn, that current cash balance would give us a cash runway of roughly 6 to 9 months. So, as we usually see with small-cap biotech, they often announce news of an equity raise alongside positive trial data.

Public Offering

So alongside the positive trial results, Corvus announced the launch of an underwritten public offering of $150 million in common stock and pre-funded warrants. There is also set to be an additional $22.5 million overallotment option available to underwriters. I think this is much needed and was expected to help the company’s balance sheet. As you’d expect, the proceeds are intended to support working capital and general corporate purposes, with management mentioning that the focus is on advancing soquelitinib.

There is an upcoming Phase 3 trial in relapsed/refractory peripheral T-cell lymphoma, and also planned Phase 2 trials in atopic dermatitis, hidradenitis suppurativa, and even asthma. The offering is being led by Jefferies and Goldman Sachs with Mizuho as bookrunner.

What's Next for Soquelitinib

Investors should now expect advancement into much larger, longer, controlled trials. We want to see durability of effect, safety, and all-around broader efficacy data readouts. Corvus is tipped to initiate a Phase 2 atopic dermatitis trial with a new 12-week dosing program and follow-up. This should tell us how sustained the skin responses are compared with the shorter Phase 1 dosing we just saw. Long-term safety and durability, especially whether deep responses like the EASI-75/90 and IGA 0/1 we saw, persist beyond treatment, are going to be critical catalysts for this stock.

If we do see the asset progress further, I am expecting this to be a commercial differentiation factor. Elsewhere, the Phase 3 T-cell lymphoma program is still ongoing, and additional indications like hidradenitis suppurativa and asthma are in much earlier stages of planning as of now.

data.title: Murphy USA: Down But Not Out
data.description:
Almost exactly a year ago, in late January 2025, I believed that Murphy USA Inc. (MUSA) made for an attractive prospect for investors. This might not seem like the most exciting prospect, especially as electric vehicles become more relevant. But as a major player in the gas station space, I viewed it as a compelling opportunity based largely on its valuation and growth prospects. I know that might seem counterintuitive, but the overall trajectory that the company should be on moving forward is promising.

Since calling it a "Buy" back then, however, the stock has underperformed, with the units falling 11.1% while the S&P 500 is up 15.7%. Financial performance has shown signs of weakness. Near term, that trend could continue. Having said that, assuming that management can follow through on its promises for additional growth, I believe that upside is sufficient to justify my previous bullish assessment.

Pulling Into Murphy USA

Given that we are in early 2026, I doubt that digging too much into financial performance for the last couple of years would be all that relevant. But for context, in the chart above, you can see financial results for 2023 and 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows largely worsened during this time. The decline in revenue came even though the number of locations that the company has in operation grew from 1,733 to 1,757. According to management, the company did see an increase in retail fuel volume from 24.8 billion gallons to 4.82 billion gallons. However, the volume per store contracted slightly, while on a same-store basis, it was nearly flat. The big problem ended up being a decline in the price of fuel. After all, oil prices and, by extension, gasoline prices have dropped some.

Naturally, you would expect to see a decline in revenue to correspond with a decline in profitability. The picture here is a bit more complicated. Operating cash flow actually grew year over year. But all the other cash flow metrics, as well as net income, worsened. That is logical. As for the 2025 fiscal year, the data that we have covers the first nine months. And, once again, the picture for the company has gotten worse.

Overall revenue for the company contracted from $15.53 billion to $14.64 billion. This was even though merchandise sales increased, growing from $3.16 billion to $3.21 billion. This is almost certainly thanks to a rise in store count from 1,740 locations to 1,772 locations. Unfortunately, petroleum product revenue dropped, falling rather sharply from $12.27 billion to $11.27 billion. Based on the data provided, this had to have been largely because of a drop in pricing. However, there was a decline in retail fuel volume during this window of time, with that metric falling from 3.62 billion gallons to 3.61 billion gallons. The fact that the firm saw a same-store sales decline during this time is discouraging. However, according to management, there has been some success in improving that. During the third quarter, for instance, the relaunch of the company’s QuickChek Rewards program helped to push food and beverage same-store sales higher. Management is also investing in certain opportunities, such as those involving food and beverage products and the center of the store offerings.

Even so, the recent weakness that we have had from a revenue standpoint has pushed profits lower. Net income dropped from $360 million in the first nine months of 2024 to $328.7 million at the same time in 2025. Operating cash flow pulled back from $598.5 million to $568.4 million. But the good news is that, if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $540 million to $575.2 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company remained flat at $728.5 million.

Despite the challenges that Murphy USA has experienced, management is optimistic about what the future holds. For starters, the company is well positioned from an end market perspective. Since the company was spun off from Murphy Oil Corporation (MUR) back in 2013, it has undergone some major transformations. It has achieved, for instance, total gallon growth of 21% during that time. A lot of this is because of the investments that the business has made in growth. However, the states in which it operates have seen total gallon growth of 7%, while the U.S. market as a whole has grown by only 4%. So geographically, the company is already advantaged.

Management has made a concerted effort to reinvent the business in other ways. This is not just about adding locations. It's also about changing the format of them. For instance, back in 2024, 28% of the 2.4 million square feet that the business had in operation was focused on its larger format locations. But by 2028, the company expects this to expand to 37% as the company grows the number of locations in operation to 1,950. In theory, these larger locations should be more economically efficient compared to other competitors that are smaller, mainly because of the benefits of economies of scale. They should also offer more merchandise and fuel options. And that, in turn, should attract more customers. And already, the company services over 2 million such individuals each day.

By 2028, management believes that it will generate around $1.30 billion worth of EBITDA. If we project our results based on what we have seen already for 2025, we would end up with a reading for that year of $1.01 billion. This would likely translate to adjusted operating cash flow of about $831.1 million and net profits of $458.8 million. But if we scale up financial performance to match estimates for 2028, we would be looking at net profits in the future of about $650.7 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $1.01 billion.

Using these estimates, the estimates that we saw for 2025, and the historical results for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. Honestly, these multiples are pretty good. But my problem comes from the fact that, even if the company achieves the kind of growth it is anticipating, annualized upside from today through the end of 2028 is just decent. In the chart below, you can see exactly what I mean here. On a price-to-earnings basis, we would be looking at an annualized upside of 12.9%. That's not bad at all considering that the stock market typically averages 10% to 11% a year. But it's not a lot of wiggle room. The price-to-adjusted-operating-cash-flow approach indicates an annualized upside of only 5.8%, while the EV to EBITDA approach indicates an upside of 11.1%.

There is, however, the opportunity for returns to be greater. You see, even as management is pushing to open additional stores, including 50 new ones this year, it has signed off on a $2 billion share buyback program. This is on top of the $1.5 billion buyback program that the company was 80% of the way through by the end of the third quarter of 2025. In fact, the company believes that, if it continues as it has, it will reduce its share count to 17 million units by the end of 2028. Management is confident that at that point, it should trade at an EV-to-EBITDA multiple of between 10 and 12. In the chart below, you can see what this would mean in terms of annualized upside for the company. At the low end, we are looking at 13.2%. At the midpoint, 17.7%. And at the high end, 21.7%. This is where the opportunity for shareholders exists. And that is enough to justify a continued bullish outlook.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I firmly believe that while there are execution-related risks and short-term economic risks to factor in, Murphy USA still justifies a bullish assessment. That's why I don't mind maintaining it as a soft "Buy" candidate at this time.

data.title: New World Order? Gold Rallies, But America's Bull Market Holds
data.description:
Is Greenland Issue Overplayed Or Is Something Troubling Brewing

The market has finally reacted to what had been a somewhat cagey month since early December. Kudos to the market reallocation warriors who saw this theme play out in the previously ignored sectors, that I also presented in a recent update. Yet, that update over the most recent weekend appears to be covered under the heap of snow that covers the most talked about island right now (which also happens to be the world's largest); Greenland.

Risk averse investors will arguably point to President Trump's latest salvo to gain control of Greenland by the "easy way or the hard way." While this has been a revolving theme since Donald Trump's first presidency, and also the early months of his second term, the threat condition has reached new heights this week. Unless you have not been reading the headlines, President Trump has headed to the World Economic Forum in Davos, as the annual confab of the top political and business leaders dissect his new tariff threats on eight countries in the EU, if the demands to allow the U.S. to purchase Greenland aren't being satisfied by Feb 10 (with the rates potentially raised to 25% by June).

If we take into account what happened in April 2025, the optimists will contend that this is a "The Art of the Deal" maneuver that comes right out of his classic playbook. Hence, the reminder that an off-ramp will somehow find its way to attendees and luminaries attending Davos with Trump to find a way to diffuse the crisis, and lead to an amicable solution before too long has passed.

However, the pessimistic political watchers will inform us that this could threaten the very fabric that enshrines the sanctity of the transatlantic alliance. And the very words of Canadian PM Mark Carney clearly highlighted why he believes that "the old order is not coming back," with the wheels already set in motion and reminding us that there's "no turning back" the clock now. President Donald Trump has doubled down on his attempt to "acquire" Greenland as he spoke at Davos on Wednesday, but he made a pivotal clarification that he "won't use force." Will that be enough to calm the markets even as though it doesn't take away what EU Chief Ursula von der Leyen cautioned that "the shift in the international order is not only seismic, but it is permanent."

For investors, dealing with shifts in geopolitical undercurrents isn't novel. The market has had to navigate such challenges since early 2022, when Russia first invaded Ukraine, as the war will soon enter its fourth year. For now, besides the recent bombing of suspected key Iranian nuclear weapon installations and the capture of Venezuelan president Nicolás Maduro, the ruckus over Greenland now represents a more substantive threat to the current international order that some might worry could be the start of the unraveling of the downward spiral toward a more dangerous world.

Shift To Gold In A New World Order?

Thus, what should investors do as we look to secure our portfolio amid the chaos? Should we wait it out as Trump's term is set to end in three years? Does that mean that the current shifts will (definitively) reverse subsequently? Should we then as some may suggest, move much if not all our assets into precious metals such as gold or silver to ride out the de-risking wave that could keep the USD and or a further advance of U.S. equity markets under pressure?

Well, there's obviously no clear answer to that. Is the market getting too risky? How do we model something that has no recent precedence? Yet, Ray Dalio offers us his perspective on the current monetary order that he spoke about a few years ago in his 2021 book.

The themes covered appear to have resonated several years later, and with the tensions further intensifying. And that worried breakdown in the monetary order could also point to having gold as not only as serving as a hedge, but also as practical and strategic considerations in the current upheavals when no one in the modern era has quite an inkling on what to really expect?

Dalio provided recent salient insights in a CNBC interview this week. He repeated his caution that the "monetary order is breaking down." As a result, we are seeing what we are witnessing right now with respect to the gold market, as it punches through to newer and newer heights, while silver has also been lifted to new heights like never seen before. The run to safety into U.S. assets isn't behaving like the way they used to, and we aren't talking about decades ago.

While the "Sell America" proposition has received swift rebukes from U.S. Secretary Scott Bessent and was also quickly refuted by Deutsche Bank CEO Christian Sewing on an analyst report by the German lender's Global Head of FX Research George Saravelos over the weekend, I don't think we are going to see the last of such "new world order" narratives. Yet, these plausible breakdowns could just be the preamble to a much worse prospect, and one that Dalios isn't mincing his words about, which is, the specter of a war, and according to him, we are now on “the brink of wars." Why? Well, the linchpin (America) that arguably provided the framework as the designer and enforcer in the post-WWII order that we know it could really be over, as he enunciated:

After World War Two…the United States was the dominant power, and it created a world order almost in its model,” he said. The result was the formation of several transnational organizations—the United Nations, the WTO and the WHO—that essentially operated as representative democracies, with the U.S. acting as the power that enforced the system. And that is no longer the case. [W]hat has happened is we've gone back to a period of time in which there is fighting for self-interest—and power rules. - WSJ CEO Brief on 21 January 2026.

U.S. Markets Have Strong Underpinnings. Don't Fret

Therefore, these have ramifications on whether U.S. exceptionalism is still a worthwhile proposition. To be clear, I've expounded these thoughts in regular updates to the members of my Investing Group: Ultimate Growth Investing, that I continue to lean structurally bullish. Why? Because the weight of the evidence in the key indicators that I've been watching hasn't convinced me that we are on the brink of a collapse. Well, I'm not saying Dalios is wrong. What I'm merely pointing out is that the market (which I believe is smarter than any one of us) hasn't given us the signal that things are as bleak as the media would want you to believe:

  • Economic growth is expected to stay resilient in 2026, and the IMF has even lifted the forecasts (up to 3.3%).
  • While the tariffs on the EU is obviously detrimental if they come to pass, it doesn't look like they will cause a Great Recession. And the signs suggest that Europe may not want to really "begin a battle that it cannot win."
  • Canada's pivot toward China to establish a strategic partnership is timely and astute. However, Canada's trade reliance on the US markets is so massive that I don't think China has the capacity nor the interest to absorb what Canada exports to the U.S. right now. What does it mean? Well, I'm not going to bet against the U.S. anytime soon. Who doesn't want to make money in the U.S. markets? Anyone? Even UBS CEO acknowledges that "diversifying" from U.S. assets is a "dangerous" narrative to undertake.

There's no doubt that by most relative measures, the U.S. market is expensive for what it's worth. With a forward earnings multiple of 22x, I concur that an advance could quickly careen to an abrupt stop if the market believes that the long-term structural underpinnings for earnings growth could be stymied.

Despite that, the current earnings outlook is still looking very rosy (corroborating the economic outlook too, right?), and AI infrastructure investments are still expected to percolate into the related supply chain and other players in the non-tech sectors. In other words, the broadening market narrative not only helps us to help mitigate near-term weaknesses in tech and growth stocks, but also provides an off ramp to take the heat off these leading plays while they consolidate for the next plausible run up.

With that in mind, I believe trying to call a bear market top in the U.S. doesn't seem to be apt in the current context, even as the break in the rules-based order could upend portfolio reallocation strategies into surging opportunities like gold. Geopolitical headwinds, while formidable, should be considered as part of the factors that could weaken the sentiments, but I beseech investors to not apply knee jerk reactions to anything that the media wants to fan the flames on at the moment.

data.title: Metallium Limited: Breaking Chinese Processing Domination, While Offering Large Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Metallium Limited (MTLMY) has been on an unstoppable rise over the past 12 months with shares up over 116%, the reference here being for the secondary ticker of MTMCF and the newly launched MTLMY ADR. I’ve covered a decent amount of mining stocks at this point and while most of them work with the mining and exploration part with each having its own niche or angle, MTMLY is different. It does have mining assets in Australia, but the main idea behind my Strong Buy right now is the processing facility in Texas, aiming to handle 8,000 tonnes per year, whilst now having secured already 2,400 tonnes from Glencore (GLCNF).

Its operating model is sound with the advantage of processing the ores differently means higher yields and lower costs. For me, this is one of the more compelling Strong Buys I will be making, since we are so close to actually seeing the first revenues hit the income statement.

A Two In One

Rather than being just another mining company Metallium has something else going for it. It’s a two in one company where it’s focusing both on clean ore processing along with exploring and developing critical mining sites. In reality, it's the first that is the most important, and the main conversation topic of this article.

The company used to be known as MTM Critical Metals in the first part of 2025 but underwent some change, perhaps even marking a pivot as not just being another mining company, but a sort of, I guess diversified mining company instead.

Flash Joule Heating - The Disruptive Tech

The first part I’ll discuss is the clean tech processing of ores and minerals by the company. It’s marketed as a green alternative to the furnace and highly chemical solutions traditionally used. I know that this is perhaps an argument as to why the company will be disruptive. But frankly, I don't ever put any serious weight to this. I don’t think it represents a MOAT, only hard financial figures showing better margins and higher profits does. Having that said, luckily the implication here is that the tech called Flash Joule heating or FJH does have a much lower operating costs than traditional methods.

The process works by there being a high voltage pulse generated which passes through the feed material. This feed could be ore, e-waste or just industrial scrap really. The shock is instant and goes to over 3,000 celsius in under a second.

The result is that the high heat generated instantly shatters the chemical and metal compounds and bonds in the feed material. This makes it either vaporize or become highly soluble. In both cases able to be washed out or as I like to put it “sieved” with a mild liquid to in the end harvest the essential parts. This is contrary to the fact that furnaces, which take many hours to produce the same product, are vastly more environmentally damaging along with having higher operating costs. These furnaces are run by met coal. Compared to just electricity the cost difference is massive.

Why I’d still say this is a disruptive technology, not because of the “green” part but instead because of the cost implication of the operating expenses. Operating costs is the toughest part for mining companies to get right, they often struggle with this, and if there is a better alternative which is both cheaper and produces higher yields, well then I’d say Metallium can be a highly disruptive play and a pivot from the “traditional” mining investments I’ve written about before.

Taking A Stab At Chinese Processing Domination

Because of China having such a monopoly still on the REE industry, even after considerable efforts by western companies, there is a need for something else.

The urban mining play that Metallium proposes is a way to pivot away from Chinese reliance and create more diversification in the processing industry. I want to make this clear before I go on, this is not about finding new REE mining sites, it’s about the processing part instead. MP Material (MP) was one of the first major companies which last year stopped sending feedstock to its Chinese partner and minority stakeholder and instead started using its Texas based processing facility.

We aim to begin commissioning in late December and effectively starting the dry commission where we'll be bumping motors, making sure everything hooks together nicely. We've given ourselves all of Q1 next year, so January, February, March for that commissioning process, just to be conservative.

Urban mining is Metallium's way of decreasing reliance on China. Urban mining just to clarify is not about opening up mining locations in suburbs, it's instead about setting up processing facilities that handles raw feedstock like ore but crucially also other feedstock types like circuits, memory boards, semiconductors. These are filled with minerals and if recovered provide a lot of revenues. It does own locations in both Australia and Canada but right now, the immediate focus is the Texas based facility which is set to commission in Q1 2026. In the following quarter the company will instead start to work on increasing the nameplate capacity instead targeting 8,000 tonnes per annum in printed circuit boards.

And then by -- we hope that by Q3, we're at that run rate of 8,000 tonnes per annum for printed circuit board feedstock. Alongside that, we're going to be commissioning our gallium germanium line as well.

This is the part that, I think, is really interesting, the two minerals Gallium and Germanium. The country had in the past banned all exports of these two minerals, but as of 2025, seems to have suspended this initial export ban now.

If you bought Gallium in 2018 you are soon about to be 10x on your investment. That is quite frankly insane when you think about it. A relatively unknown or perhaps underutilized mineral like this can appreciate this much in value purely because of rising demand, but with the cherry on top, the export bans and restrictions from the country controlling the majority of the vital mineral.

It’s a very interesting mineral as it could melt in your hand but is right now used in combination with nitrogen and then used in EV fast-chargers or 5G base stations. But why the US might want a bigger piece of it has to do with its use in military radars. With the US budget potentially growing by 50% until next year, it further supports increased prices for these minerals as the money is being allocated to pay whatever the current market price is, no matter what.

E-Waste, The Place To Be?

In February 2025, MTM demonstrated ~90% and ~80% recovery rates for Gallium and Germanium respectively from semiconductor metal refining waste.

In one of the recent reports from the company, they include some figures on recent FJH performance figures. This is part of a broader e-waste business model the company is pushing. It has included figures on the economics of this, and it’s quite frankly very appealing.

20% is feedstock costs, like semiconductors and other stuff. Then there is about a 15% loss from recovery anticipated and a 15% loss in payability. But Metallium argues it can achieve a 50% gross margin on this model. The semiconductor stream is what will bring the gallium and germanium. Then you have operating and processing expenses. Metallium expects 87% lower energy consumption compared to Hydromet processing. Other advantages include 37% higher yield per tonne along with 69% lower input costs compared to Hydromet plus remote mining.

But the story here is not just about these two items. Yes, they are big ticket items, but when the company also reports a 100% recovery in Gold and Titanium, or 97% for Silver and Tin then you have to realize this: it's not just about rare earth minerals anymore, but instead about being a leading recovery company offering diversification away from China, and instead into the arms of the US.

Mining Exploration

The mining exploration part of Metallium concerns in large parts, mostly the Australian footprint it has, then anything else really. It’s not a story about rare earths here, something which Australia has plenty of in terms of identified reserves. The country ranks fourth with 3.8 million tonnes found so far. Rather, the headline should be the Niobium. The mineral concerns the West Arunta project, which is directly adjacent to WA1 Resources. This company made one of the biggest discoveries of the precious minerals in the past 70 years.

The mineral is used to solve the bottleneck in steel production, helping make it either stronger or lighter basically. Now, Metallium has not yet found this yet at their project, but being so close, basically a neighbour to one that did unarguably made their chances just so much better. It's only my opinion that Metallium could possible mine the mineral here, but I have to make it clear they have not yet found any.

If Metallium announces it has found carbonatite which is the rock that holds the metal, then this segment of the business might be what makes the stock shoot up even more. It could trump the processing side if the quantity is high enough. The price has since 2021 increased by about 62%. It’s not the crazy growth that REE has seen, it is still considerable.

Glencore De-Risking

When I say de-risking I mean it. The recently signed agreement with Glencore makes the ambitious plan of setting up large recovery facilities across the US over the long-term a reality, and most definitely ensures that the short-term goals will be met when talking about the first Texas facility commissioning.

This multi-year contract guarantees access to 2,400 tonnes per annum of electronic scrap, providing the feedstock certainty needed to commission and scale the company's revolutionary Flash Joule Heating (FJH) technology in Texas.

The 8,000 in nameplate capacity that Metallium has been aiming for in Q3/Q4 of this year just secured about 30% of its feedstock with this deal. The business falls apart if it can’t get this feedstock into the plant and start generating revenues. Important to know is that the contract will be running until the end of this year. It’s not a multi-year deal, but a way to secure feedstock now in the initial start-up phase, but I would guess with a lot of potential to be renewed if Metallium is able to execute its plan this year.

With feedstock secured, the only other question you should have is “what about the funding?”. Well, Metallium has got this sorted out as well. Back in December 2025, it secured a partnership with ElementUSA which includes $10.1 million which is non-dilutive as well. I find this dilution part extremely important seeing as we are so close to actually starting to produce revenues, and a massive share count increase prior to this could sour the market mood. The company also has about $50 million cash in the bank as well. Combined, the $60.1 million or so here is enough to cover operating costs ($19.9 million TTM) for the next 3 years. If based on the operating cash flow and capital expenditures ($3.1 million and $600,000) the cash covers the operations for a longer period, 16.2 years to be specific. This gets us well within when Metallium should actually start producing revenues and scale up to 8,000 tonnes per year.

Revenue Model

So effectively meaning that of the contained metal value, we're aiming to capture 40% of that to our bottom line.

This quote comes directly from the CEO Michael Walshe when the company presented at one of Morgan Stanley's events back in November. No doubt the market is excited when a mining company comes out and says they are aiming for 40% net margins when others like MP or Lynas Rare Earths Limited (LYSCF) have a hard time even being profitable. MP, for instance, has reported a net loss for over 2 years straight and Lynas reported just above $5 million in net income with $366 million in revenues on a TTM basis.

Seeing as the company is still pre-revenue, I think the most reasonable approach is to make a “waterfall” approach here for the revenues and earnings. Essentially, we are looking at the input cost which, in this case, is “metals of board” using a $28,000 price per tonne here. This table is highly diverse, meaning it basically uses a combined yield rather than breaking out specifics, like 100% for gold and titanium.

It’s impossible to know the actual mix of what feedstock Metallium will be using, so this does act as a very nice way of gauging the revenue potential. Speaking of this, based on the 8,000 tonne target the annual operating income would be $145 million and $80.1 million in net margins. For a company with an EV of $385 million it does suggest there is a lot of upside potential here in the short to medium term just based off of this.

This table here instead looks at the semiconductor scraps which would start in Q3 at the same time as the line for Gallium and Germanium would start. Because of the higher concentration of these in semiconductors the table changes dramatically and so does the revenue profile, $362 million in operating income and $272 million in net income. This, of course, is at the moment quite unlikely as getting 8,000 tonnes per year of this stuff is hard, and it’s not just Metallium recycling, there are others. But it does show the impact that appreciating mineral prices has on the net income as much of the potential growth of those two, or any other for that matter, almost directly hits the bottom line of Metallium.

Metallium will, of course, not be operating with that capacity of 8,000 tonnes straight out of the gate. Just based on the first table and the secured feedstock from Glencore implies $43.6 million in operating income and $24.03 million in net income. I’m very comfortable buying this at 20x earnings because of the growth implications here. This is even below the broader Materials sector it’s in. At 20x the estimated earnings figure I arrive at a market cap of $480 million. I won’t make any models here pointing to a billion dollar valuation, I think it’s too early. But I would lie if I said I don’t believe it could ever reach that, and fast too.

Downside Risk

The main downside risk for Metallium and most of the mining companies I’ve covered which are pre-revenue is bottlenecks in the construction phase. Any slowdowns or setbacks is going to remarkably hurt the stock price in the short term and create a lot of volatility. Those who have held the stock since a year ago wouldn't care too much about this considering how it has moved, but for newer investors it's a risk worth considering. This can then carry over into more share dilution as equity is needed to fund the operations.

The second risk is how tied the stock price of Metallium will be to commodities. Changes in global exports and bans like what China has done is going to have a more noticeable impact as well. This outsources the risks so to speak, and that is not always a good thing. It means the company has less control of performing well financially in simple terms. It has less control of what the net earnings for a certain quarter would be. That will be set by whatever the net selling price of their end products are, and there Metallium has little bargaining power, it’s whatever the market says it is.

The Full Picture

Metallium is a very exciting play right now as a mineral processing company showcasing industry-leading yields and low operating costs as well. The stock has been on an absolute surge the past 12 months but still shows upside based on my relatively conservative estimates of $480 million market cap. Its financials are in order with $50 million in cash and $10.1 million in secured non-dilutive loans. It’s a Strong Buy. This is a long-term investment based more on changing REE processing away from China and into the west, whilst benefiting from higher commodity prices.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: How Inspiring New CEO Will Take Fight To Key Rival Eli Lilly In 2026
data.description:
Investment Overview

Last night, I sat down and read through the transcript of Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) CEO Maziar Doustdar's "fireside chat" at the JP Morgan (JPM) Healthcare Conference on January 13th. Having listened to it again today, I am convinced, firstly, that it may be the most impactful interview given by any Pharma CEO in 2026, and secondly, that it will have a profound impact on Novo's share price and valuation in 2026.

In this article I am going to explain why.

Background To Novo, Semaglutide, & Pharma's "Annus Horribilis" In 2025

In February last year, I purchased stock in Novo, when it was valued at ~$85 per share. I then watched on in disbelief as it plunged in value, reaching a low of ~$45 per share in early August - a loss of of >45%.

It was Novo stock's lowest ebb for four years, but between August 2021 and August 2024, stock rose in value from $45, to $140 - up >210%. This was largely due to the runaway success of a single drug molecule - semaglutide.

Semaglutide is a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist , approved to treat patients with Type 2 Diabetes ("T2D") in December 2017, brand name Ozempic, and then approved to treat patients with obesity in June 2021, brand name Wegovy.

The drug is famed for its ability to help patients lose weight - >15% at 72 weeks at the lower dose, and using the more recently tested higher dose (7.5mg), >20% at 72 weeks. Prior to the arrival of semaglutide, the scientific community had not believed that such dramatic weight loss was achievable, but the drug's unique abilities to stimulate insulin release, reduce glucagon, slow stomach emptying, and decrease appetite, have proven ground-breaking.

In 2022, Ozempic earned revenues of ~$8.3bn, and Wegovy ~$860m. In 2023, Ozempic revenue grew to ~$13.3bn, and Wegovy revenues to ~$4.4bn. In 2024, the figures were ~$16.8bn and ~$8bn, and in 2025, although final figures have not been shared, based on performance to the end of Q3, I'd estimate Ozempic will earn ~$19.6bn of revenues, and Wegovy ~$11.8bn.

Together, these two drugs will account for ~66% of Novo total revenues (which I estimate will be ~$48bn) in 2025. Wegovy sales will be up ~45% year-on-year, and Ozempic sales up ~17%. Novo's total revenues will be up ~18% year-on-year.

Given these numbers - and analysts (credible, in my view) estimates that semaglutide could achieve close to $60bn in peak annual revenues in 2031, readers may be asking "crisis, what crisis?". How does a Pharma growing revenues at >15% per annum, while marketing and selling what may be the world's best-selling prescription drug, lose ~65% of its share price value in less than 12 months?

I have discussed the reasons why in previous posts on Novo. The underwhelming performance of another GLP-1 drug class candidate, Cagrisema, in clinical studies is one. The imposition of tariffs on imports into the U.S. announced by President Trump last April another, and the ongoing campaign to introduce a "most favored nation" ("MFN") pricing policy in the U.S., and impose tariffs specifically on pharma imports a third.

A fourth, and possibly most important factor - Novo's rivalry with Eli Lilly (LLY) and its tirzepatide molecule - another member of the GLP-1 drug class, approved to treat T2D as Mounjaro, and obesity as Zepbound, with weight loss data just as impressive (and perhaps even marginally superior to) semaglutide.

Then there was downgraded guidance (when announcing Q2 earnings), compounded drug manufacturers making "knock-off" versions of semaglutide and exploiting regulatory loopholes to sell them via platforms such as Hims and Hers (HIMS), and the unexpected departure of the Pharma's longstanding CEO, Lars Fruergaard Jorgensen, in May.

Why I Am So Energized By New CEO's Comments At JPM Healthcare Conference Part 1 - Narrowing The Focus

Into the vacant CEO position stepped Maziar Doustdar, who, according to his Wikipedia profile, "joined Novo Nordisk in 1992 as an office clerk in Vienna."

It seems very clear to me that for a company with Novo's credentials to have lost 65% of its share price value in less than 12 months, management must have been doing a lot of things wrong, and during his recent fireside chat several things struck me about the approach of the new CEO that I found immediately impressive / intriguing.

First of all, one thing Novo failed to do (in my view) in 2024 and 2025 was to control the narrative and hype around the GLP-1 drug class and its potential. While analysts shared forecasts for a >$125bn market by 2029, and hype around GLP-1 drugs reached fever pitch, Novo committed a cardinal sin by over-promising, and under-delivering.

In my view there was no need to promise the market that Cagrisema would achieve >25% weight loss, when the figures achieved by semaglutide were already impressive enough, yet that it was Novo did. When Cagrisema came up short, the market had a legitimate reason to dump Novo stock.

Consider these comments from the new CEO, however:

Novo has a 103-year history. And then of course, we are mainly synonymous for doing a couple of things, but doing it better than anyone else. The company has always been about diabetes and remains so. We have more recently moved into the obesity space innovating in that front, and that is also remains very much intact.

Then, of course, when you take the 2 large chronic disease of diabetes and obesity, you recognize that patients suffering from these conditions often have many other issues, comorbidities related to them, chronic kidney disease, CVD, MASH and the likes.

And we have been moving into these adjacencies the last few years more and more. And then we will continue doing that. But with one fine difference, I would say, and that is that the starting point for us will have to be focused around a patient, that's either suffering from high blood sugar, so diabetes or high weight obesity.

In summary, I believe the CEO is saying "let's not focus on what we don't do and the drugs we don't yet sell, let's focus on what we are doing with the drugs we do sell, because we are doing it better than anyone else".

To put it another way, why should Novo's valuation be based around the potential of Cagrisema, when the company has semaglutide, a drug whose phenomenal progress drove a >200% gain in its share price between 2021 and 2024?

If Novo earns ~$48bn of revenues in 2025 as per my estimate, forward price to sales ratio will be ~5.6x, and if we take nine-month earnings per share ("EPS") of $2.7, and average out for the full year - ~$3.6, we arrive at a price to earnings ("P/E") ratio of ~17x.

Now let's compare this to Eli Lilly, which has a market cap valuation of $985bn at present, a trailing 12m P/S ratio of ~16x (based on full-year revenue forecast of ~$63bn), and P/E ratio of ~51x.

Yes, Eli Lilly has a wider and more diverse product range than Novo, but make no mistake, its share price gains of >400% on a five-year basis have been driven almost exclusively by tirzepatide, or Zepbound / Mounjaro, a drug that is far more similar to semaglutide than it is differentiated.

For anybody who believes narrowing the focus at Novo may result in lower revenue growth / overdependence on obesity / T2D, CEO Maziar Doustdar's take is as follows:

For Novo Nordisk, we have realized that we are really good when we focus. So the focus, again, will go back to obesity and diabetes. And for those who then will get a little bit nervous, just a couple of things. I'll remind them that those couple of things have 2 billion people suffering from those conditions. So this is a very, very large unmet need where we and our competition today are only touching the surface when it comes to treatment of these individuals.

Why I Am So Energized By New CEO's Comments At JPM Healthcare Conference Part 2 - Admitting Mistakes, Making Changes

CEO's at large companies typically do not like to admit to making mistakes - all too often, it will result in their being relieved of their jobs, but in CEO Doustdar's case, there is a unique opportunity for someone who started out as an office clerk to admit some culpability, clear the decks, and adjust the strategy.

Here is what the CEO had to say about Novo's disastrous 2025 during his fireside chat:

So first, I'll come up with not an excuse, but a bit of a defense on 2025. '25 was a difficult year. It was difficult for Novo Nordisk, has been difficult for our shareholders. So I will not belittle that one bit.

But I will say, it's the curse of a leader. We went into obesity some 30 years ago. And at the time -- and up until more recently, I would say, many of our peers that have sat in this stage now or will sit here in the future were making fun of us.

They basically thought obesity is not a disease. Obesity, there is no money in it. Obesity is something that should be left to individual patients to get on with it, get on with your lifestyle, get on with your exercise and diet and kind of -- this is not for pharma to go there.

To me, this take feels entirely right. Given Novo was largely responsible for creating today's >$60bn per annum GLP-1 drug market, perhaps the company deserves more credit from Mr. Market, especially given it also continues to shape the future of this industry?

However, the new CEO is also humble enough to admit that Novo cannot afford to rest on its laurels:

we're no longer on our own, and we need to hurry up and it cannot be like the previous couple of years where we were left alone just because we had a head start.

On the hindsight, when I look back, we could have done better. And it's easy for those who are following us to correct our mistakes and make sure that they do a better version of it.

The CEO then goes on to discuss the promotion of Wegovy / Ozempic, admitting to some hair-raising oversights:

to hear 55 million people have insurance was really exciting. Only then to get into the details and realize that's a bit theoretical. All the pre-authorizations, the obstacles that the insurance companies have created for many of these millions means that it's not that they're going to be able to get their medicine just because they're obese and/or have insurance.

Doustdar is admitting to a misjudgment - Novo believed all it had to do was manufacture and ship Wegovy / Ozempic, and patients would flock to buy it, while insurers would make it easy for them to do so.

We now know that is not the case - the cash market may emerge as king, fueled by DTC platforms - similar, in fact, to those platforms that were so successful making compounded semaglutide available to cash buyers in 2025.

The CEO told the audience on Jan 13th:

We have relaunched our NovoCare Pharmacy on the back of the, what you call, the Wegovy pill. I'm very optimistic about that, but we also realize that that's not enough future. We need to meet the patients where they are. So we have gone into a massive amount of partnerships with Ro, LifeMD, Amazon, WeightWatchers, Costco and really making sure that we make our products available.

Us and Lilly combined have probably 10 million, 15 million patients. What about the other 85 million, we need to get to them.

Why I Am So Energized By New CEO's Comments At JPM Healthcare Conference Part 3 - Taking On Lilly, It's Biggest Rival

While Novo stock plunged in 2025, and the Pharma became engulfed in crisis, Lilly's progress was serene - shares gained ~40% in 2025, and it became the first trillion-dollar valuation Pharma. By my calculation, tirzepatide will earn revenues of ~$35bn in 2025, making it the world's best-selling prescription drug - but only narrowly ahead of semaglutide.

Now, let's consider the competitive landscape in 2026. Importantly, both Lilly and Novo have secured drug pricing deals with the Trump Administration (see my November 2025 note for more detail), agreeing to drop the prices of Ozempic / Wegovy and Zepbound / Mounjaro from ~$1.3k per month, to ~$350 (if purchased via Trump Rx, although I'd expect Novo's DTC channel to offer a similar price).

Crucially, however, Novo has made it to market first with an oral version of the self-injectable Wegovy, which was approved on 22nd December 2025, while Lilly's approval decision for its oral GLP-1, orforglipron, has been delayed, from 28th March, to April 10th.

Furthermore, oral Wegovy arguably looks like the superior drug - the pill showed a mean weight loss of 16.6% in its pivotal OASIS 4 trial, while orforglipron achieved a figure of 7.9%. Yes, oral Wegovy will be sold at a discount - ~$145 for non-insured patients, and as low as $25 for insured patients - but the market is so vast, revenue growth remains the logical outcome.

Back to CEO Doustdar's fireside chat, and I love the fight and attitude shown when discussing oral Wegovy versus the competition. Firstly, on tolerability:

Then when you look at it from a tolerability point of view, when you look at our clinical trials, some 7% of the trial participants left us due to tolerability issues. When you look at my competitors, it was around at the highest dose from 22% to 24% that left.

On the potential need for patients to fast prior to taking oral Wegovy:

we have about 1.5 million patients to the last count I had, on Rybelsus, which is the same drug in the diabetes segment. And we have not seen this being an issue with 1.5 million people. So I beg to think that this should be the same with Wegovy pill.

And on injectables versus orals:

My father in law has been very much waiting for an oral, even though he has been needing and wanting to take a GLP-1. The reason he actually did not want to go for Wegovy has been, he didn't feel like he should get an injection. I think there's a societal taboo on injection.

There's also the element of refrigeration. I have a friend in Montana, who basically is -- my competition's drug and mentioned to me the other day that wants to switch to Wegovy pill. And I have to say I was surprised. Why do you want to do that? And I first thought, it was just because he's my friend. But that was not the case. This gentleman travels a lot. And he's simply too nervous about all the time having kept the medication in cold chain.

Firstly, these are important and accurate points, and secondly, I cannot help but admire the subtle swipes taken against Novo's biggest (and in fact, at present, only) competitor - the almighty Eli Lilly.

Finally, on the potency of semaglutide versus tirzepatide and the use of larger doses:

our company is also a bit conservative when it comes to these things. We really feel that we should not overdose patients unless -- it's just -- so we thought 2.4 milligrams, 15%, 16%, 3x Saxenda, fantastic. We went in that direction and the product came out. And we then did a lot of studies around heart, kidney and took it from a very much diabetes muscle memory that we had and developed semaglutide and brought it to the market.

I cannot speak to what my competition has done. I can only imagine that when you are second to market and you're designing your trials, then you look at whatever is existing. So let's assume that Novo Nordisk product has 15%, 16%, and you want to get to 20%, 21%, then you tell yourself well, I've got to go all the way up to 15-milligram of my product to get there and then you design your trials and then you show that you're efficacious.

The world, in my opinion, has a little bit misread this.

The world, in my opinion, has a little bit misread how to value Novo Nordisk's business, but I expect this to change in 2026.

Concluding Thoughts: A Bullish New Approach From Novo, But Can It Drag Share Price Out Of The Doldrums?

This is the million, or rather $1 trillion, dollar question - you have two companies, Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk, built on the strength of their diabetes / obesity franchises, duking it out for market supremacy.

Internationally, Novo claims to have ~68% of the market, while in the U.S., Lilly claims to have nosed ahead. In that sense, the fact that Lilly is worth >3x more than Novo in terms of market cap feels almost laughable, however I have cautioned before that the market is rarely rational, and arguably, Lilly's share price can keep growing.

Lilly arguably has the lead in terms of manufacturing, committing to spend ~$27bn in the U.S. - although in a post President Trump world, if international tariffs are removed, manufacturing in the US may prove to be a costly error.

Novo, meanwhile, has been in a mess operationally, but has reacted by appointing a new CEO, and the signs are this new boss is not at all the same as the old boss.

President Trump arguably rose to prominence in the U.S. political world thanks to his ability to deliver hard truths, highlighting what he felt was wrong with economy, international relations etc. and explaining how he would fix it, and not being afraid to take on his rivals head-on, as opposed to obliquely.

While I would not remotely compare Novo's new CEO with the U.S. President, it does strike me that Doustdar is not afraid to criticize his own company's performance if necessary, to pick fights with his rivals, and to focus on what "makes Novo great," rather than concerning himself with what may make it not so great.

It's a refreshing attitude that immediately struck me as I went through the transcript from the JP Morgan conference, and arguably, much-needed after the debacle that was the first eight months of 2025 for the company.

It is, however, a double edged sword. Making enemies within you own company by criticizing past performance is not always wise, and neither is admitting to mistakes, i.e., Novo's lack of understanding of the U.S. insurance industry. If you made that mistake yesterday, what stops you making it tomorrow?

Nevertheless, I found many of the new CEO's arguments persuasive. Oral Wegovy is best-in-class and reached >3k patients in its first week on the market. Novo needed to adjust its marketing strategy and has done so.

Novo will report earnings and likely provide 2026 guidance on February 4th, and I'd expect management to forecast another year of double-digit revenue growth, and EPS growth, and I am confident that will be a catalyst for share price gains, as will the performance of oral Wegovy and pipeline progress - let's not forget, Novo is the original, and arguably still the best at GLP-1 drug development.

As such, I believe it would not be unrealistic to see stock mount a recovery and trade somewhere between $75 - $100 by year end. That represents upside at the midpoint of >30%, running ahead of what I'd expect to be ~10-15% annual revenue / EPS growth, but, starting from such a low ebb, I'd expect share price gains to run ahead of company performance - provided the new CEO can truly steady the ship operationally and demonstrate his commercial savvy.

The company is right to focus on its core strengths of diabetes and now obesity (if you want to read an excellent analysis of how Novo may augment this strategy with strategic M&A, I'd refer you here), given this market, despite already being the biggest in pharmaceutical history, is still in its infancy.

Luckily, in the obesity space, it is not the case that you must buy Novo and sell Lilly, or sell Novo and buy Lilly. You can own both, and they may even be good for one another, as Pepsi's rivalry with Coke is arguably good for both companies, for example.

Obesity / GLP-1 drugs was the biggest pharmaceutical story of 2024, and 2025, and I fully expect it to be the biggest pharmaceutical show in town in 2026, also. Under its new CEO, I expect Novo to take the fight to Lilly in way it was unable to do last year. In short - let's get ready to rumble!
